ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for Singaporean white bee hoon. FOODON_03315595 (rice vermicelli dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0GJM covers cooked rice noodles as a category. No specific code exists for Singaporean white bee hoon with seafood and broth. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Singaporean noodle dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One combo serving consists of two full plates of white bee hoon, each approximately 350 g, totalling approximately 700 g for two persons sharing.

nutrient_source.title: Rice vermicelli with seafood, cooked — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) Foodo; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB Foodo entry exists for 'Signature White Bee Hoon' as a branded restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated by compositing: (1) HPB Foodo entry for 'Fried Bee Hoon with Seafood' (code SG-FBH-SF) as the closest Singaporean analogue for the noodle-seafood base, and (2) USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked rice vermicelli (NDB 20065) and mixed seafood components. The white bee hoon preparation is characterised by a milky broth base (lard, stock, white pepper) rather than a dark soy sauce fry, resulting in slightly lower sodium and slightly higher fat than a standard fried bee hoon. Values are adjusted accordingly and represent a best-estimate per 100 g of the combined dish. Confidence is medium due to absence of a direct branded-dish laboratory analysis.

gi_source.title: Rice vermicelli, boiled — Glycemic Index Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singaporean white bee hoon as a branded restaurant dish. The GI of 53 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled rice vermicelli (GI = 53, low category), which is the primary carbohydrate component of this dish. The addition of seafood, egg, lard, and broth lowers the overall meal GI further due to protein and fat co-ingestion; however, the rice vermicelli GI of 53 is used as a conservative upper-bound estimate for the dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 17.5 g per 100 g × 7.0 serving factor) / 100 ≈ 65.5 g GL for the full 700 g combo. gi_category set to 'low' as GI 53 ≤ 55 per Veritas threshold rules.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 420 mg per 100 g; a full 350 g single-person serving delivers approximately 1,470 mg sodium, representing about 64% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a serving and request reduced-sodium broth preparation, as the cumulative sodium from the seafood, soy sauce, and stock base can contribute substantially to daily sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice vermicelli has a GI of 53 (low) but the carbohydrate load per full serving is high; a single 350 g portion delivers approximately 61 g of carbohydrate, which requires careful portioning for glycaemic management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a half-portion (approximately 175 g) and pair it with additional non-starchy vegetables to moderate the glycaemic response, as the total carbohydrate load of a full serving is substantial despite the low GI of the noodle base.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat content from lard and cholesterol from seafood and egg; a full single-person serving provides approximately 5.6 g saturated fat and approximately 238 mg cholesterol.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request oil-reduced preparation and limit consumption to occasional meals, as the lard-based broth and mixed seafood contribute to saturated fat and dietary cholesterol intake that may adversely affect lipid profiles.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Signature White Bee Hoon Combo (2 Servings) is a Singaporean Chinese noodle dish consisting of white rice vermicelli (bee hoon) cooked in a rich, milky-white broth made from lard, garlic, ginger, and seafood or chicken stock. The combo format serves two persons and is a signature offering of The Seafood Market (House of White Bee Hoon) restaurant group in Singapore. The dish is characterised by its pale, opaque broth — a deliberate contrast to the dark soy-based fried bee hoon more commonly found in hawker centres — and is typically loaded with fresh seafood including prawns, squid, and clams, along with egg and spring onion. The texture of the noodles is soft and silky, having absorbed the umami-rich broth during the braising and simmering process. The flavour profile is savoury, mildly sweet from the seafood, and fragrant from white pepper and garlic, with a subtle richness from the lard. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (approximately 138 kcal per 100 g), a meaningful protein contribution from the mixed seafood and egg (approximately 7.2 g per 100 g), and moderate carbohydrate from the rice vermicelli base (approximately 17.5 g per 100 g). The GI of the rice vermicelli component is 53 (low), though the total carbohydrate load per full serving is substantial. Sodium content is elevated due to the broth and seasoning, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to the seafood, egg, and lard content, and contains crustacean and mollusc allergens. It is a culturally significant dish in the Singapore seafood dining scene, often ordered as a shared meal for families or groups.
common_user_queries: How many calories in white bee hoon?
What is the GI of bee hoon?
Is white bee hoon suitable for diabetics?
What seafood is in white bee hoon?
What is the difference between white bee hoon and fried bee hoon?
How much sodium is in white bee hoon?

confidence_reason: No direct laboratory analysis or HPB Foodo entry exists for this specific branded restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated by compositing HPB Foodo data for the closest Singaporean analogue (fried bee hoon with seafood) and USDA SR Legacy data for individual components, with adjustments for the white broth preparation method. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled rice vermicelli as the primary carbohydrate source. Confidence is medium rather than low because the estimation methodology is systematic and grounded in approved primary sources.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates; no direct branded-dish laboratory analysis is available. Values should be updated if HPB Foodo or an equivalent approved source publishes a direct entry for white bee hoon. 2. The combo format (x2) represents two full single-person servings totalling approximately 700 g. Per-100 g values are calculated on the basis of the combined dish weight. 3. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy source data used for this estimation. 4. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the source data used for this estimation. 5. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the source data used for this estimation. 6. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible in the rice vermicelli and seafood components per USDA SR Legacy data. 7. The dish is not certified halal; lard is a traditional ingredient in the white broth preparation at the originating restaurant. 8. GI category is 'low' (GI = 53, ≤ 55 per Veritas threshold rules). 9. Glycemic load per serving (65.5) is calculated for the full 700 g combo (2 servings); per single serving GL ≈ 32.8. 10. FoodOn URI FOODON_03315595 and FoodEx2 code A0GJM are used as closest parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for this specific dish.
